summary: McKenzie describes his most ambitious A/B test: removing Bingo Card Creator's downloadable desktop application entirely and funneling all users to the web version. After finding that 78% of sales come from web app users while the desktop version generates 10x more support emails, he built an A/B test where half of visitors see a site where the download never existed. He expects a 25% sales increase by converting download users to web signups, which convert at twice the rate. The test was carefully designed to not disrupt existing customers, with override mechanisms and strategic summer timing.
key_points: The downloadable version generates 10x more support emails but only accounts for roughly 25% of sales, making it a net drag on the business
Rather than simply dropping the product, McKenzie A/B tests the removal to measure whether the impact is 'modestly positive or extraordinarily positive'
Higher conversion rates create a compounding flywheel with AdWords: better conversion lets you bid more per click, which gets more traffic, which generates more sales
Customers don't understand the distinction between desktop and web apps, so removing the choice simplifies their decision and likely increases total conversion
Strategic timing matters: launching during summer's sales crater means fewer customers are affected while kinks get worked out
money_quotes: Essentially, I'm A/B testing dropping a profitable product which has a modest bit of traction and thousands of paying customers.

I get literally ten support emails about the downloadable application for every one I get about the web application, and one of the first things I suggest to customers is 'Try using the web version, it will magically fix that.'
The last year has taught me quite a bit about the difference between web applications and downloadable applications. To whit: don't write desktop apps.
I think offering two choices probably confuses customers and decreases the total conversion rate. Eliminating one might help.
The A/B test is mostly to show me whether it is modestly positive or extraordinarily positive.
Darn, that is a bloody brilliant business model, where they extract rent every time I do hard work.
I can't allow myself to stay married to all old decisions just out of a desire to be consistent.
key_insight: When a legacy product generates disproportionate support costs and lower conversion rates than its replacement, A/B testing its complete removal can reveal that killing it actually grows the business.
